div.p: The reference gamma lines tool shows energies of gamma and x-rays emitted by an isotope, both in a table within the tool and also as lines on the spectrum.

A key part of performing an anlysis with InterSpec is associating peaks with specific energy gamma or x-rays from a nuclide - the easiest way to accomplish this is to display reference lines for a nuclide before fitting the peaks. When reference lines are displayed, and a peak is fit, if there is a reference line reasonably near the fit peak, then that peak will be assigned that energy gamma or x-ray. Once this is done, that peak can be used for energy calibration, or for fitting source activity, age, or shielding.

Options for using the tool:
Nuclide to display: All non-stable nuclides are available for display, as well as fluorescent for elements, many nuclear reactions, and some other sources; see "Sources Available To Display" section below for more info.
Age: For isotopes where aging will have an effect on the distribution of gamma and/or x-ray amplitudes, age of the nuclide can be specfied using a combination of time units, similar to "5.3y 8d 22m" or in half lives like "2.5 HL". Acceptable time units: year, yr, y, day, d, hrs, hour, h, minute, min, m, second, s, ms, microseconds, us, nanoseconds, ns, or you can specify time period by hh:mm:ss. Half life units can be specified using hl, halflife, halflives, half-life, half-lives, half lives, or half life.
Half life units or time periods can not be mixed with other units.
When multiple time periods are specified, they are summed, e.x. "1y6months 3m" is interpreted as 18 months and 3 minutes.
The initial age used when a nuclide is entered is based on a heuristic that generally works well, but in the case of freshly produced, or extensively aged nuclides may need to be adjusted.
Adjusting the age is not allowed for nuclides whose signatures do not significantly change with age.
Prompt Only: For nuclides that have decay chains where at some point a child half-life is longer than its parents, selecting this option will cause only the nuclides in the decay chain down to that point to be included in the reference lines shown, with them all being in static equilibrium.
This option is only shown for nuclides where it applies, like U-238, Th-232, etc.
Color: You can select the color you would like the reference lines to appear on the spectrum - this will also cause peaks fit while showing the reference lines that can be attributed to the nuclide, to be that color as well. This is useful to quickly identify which peaks are attributed to different nuclides.
Detector Response Function: If a detector response function (DRF) is selected then the amplitude of lines drawn on the spectrum, will be convoluted with the DRF so that lines your detector has a lower sensitivity to will be shown with a smaller amplitude.
The DRF does not effect the B.R. shown in the tools table.
Shielding: Because shielding can drastically alter the expected distribution of gammas and x-rays, you can enter a shielding type and thickness to have the reference lines shown on the spectrum be modulated by the attenuation of that shielding.
Shielding does not effect the B.R. shown in the tools table.

By default, reference lines for x-ray (dashed lines) and gammas are shown. However, if you select the sub menu, to the right of the color select (see the circled in the below image), you can select to see alpha energies, beta endpoints, true-coincidence sums (i.e., when two gammas are emitted at nearly same time, so may be summed by the detector), as well as a few other options.

To show reference lines for multiple nuclides at once:
Enter first nuclide, and adjust all options (shielding, age, DRF).
Click the "Add Another" button, at which point the nuclide field will be cleared of the current nuclide; however the first nuclides lines will still be showing on the spectrum
Enter the second nuclide and adjust all options. Lines for both nuclides will be showing on the spectrum, but the tools table will only show values for the most recently entered spectrum.
Click "Add Another", and repeat for all wanted nuclides.
Click the "Clear" button to remove the lines for all nuclides.
div.div:
In addition to displaying gammas for any non-stable nuclide, you can also display:
Element x-rays - type the element symbol (or the element name) into the "Nuclide" field, e.g. "U", "Pb", "Fe". The fluorescence x-rays for that element will be shown. You may also append "xray" or "x-ray" (e.g. "U xray").
Background - type "background" to show the gamma and x-ray lines from a representative natural background composed of K-40, Th-232, U-238, U-235, and Ra-226 (with the latter four aged appropriately and transported through soil).
Custom energies - type a number with energy units (e.g. "661 keV" or "1.46 MeV") to display a single line at that energy. Useful when you just want a marker at a specific energy. If "Show Escape Peaks" is enabled and the energy exceeds 1022 keV, the single and double-escape lines are also shown.
Nuclear reactions - type the reaction in the form "Target(in,out)", e.g. "H(n,g)", "Fe(n,g)", "Be9(a,n)", "B10(n,a)", "N14(a,p)", "Fe(n,n)", "Fe(a,a)". Particle codes are n = neutron, a = alpha, p = proton, g = gamma. Supported reaction types are (a,n), (n,a), (a,p), (n,g) (neutron capture), (n,n) (neutron inelastic scatter), and (a,a) (alpha inelastic scatter).
Fission product spectra - type "Fission nuclide type, buildup", e.g. "Fission U235 Thermal, 1d buildup", "Fission U233 Fast, 1d buildup", "Fission U238 14 MeV, 1h buildup", or for spontaneous fission either "Cf252 + Spontaneous Fission" or "Fission Cf252 Spontaneous". Fission types are Thermal, Fast, 14 MeV, and Spontaneous. After the fission event, the resulting fission products decay (and ingrow) for the specified buildup time, and the resulting gamma/x-ray spectrum is shown. Independent fission yields are available for U-233, U-235, U-238, Pu-239, Pu-241, Np-237, Am-241, Cm-244, Cm-248, and Cf-252. The "Age" entered in the tool is the time since fission stopped (e.g., how long the built up fission products are decayed for).
Built-in U / Pu isotopic mixtures - the file data/add_ref_line.xml ships several preset named mixtures you can type directly into the "Nuclide" field, including "U depleted", "U natural", "U 3% enriched", "Pu low burnup", "Pu high burnup", and "Pu heat source". See "Adding Your Own Sources" below for more.
Built-in named source-line sets - also defined in data/add_ref_line.xml: "U-iso" and "Pu-iso" provide curated sets of recommended uranium and plutonium peaks (typically used as starting points for isotopic analysis), and "HPGe(n,n)" provides germanium-detector inelastic-scatter lines.
User-defined sources - you can add your own named single-source line lists or nuclide mixtures that show up alongside the built-in ones. See "Adding Your Own Sources" below.

div.p: The list of sources you can enter into the "Nuclide" field on this tab is not limited to the built-in nuclides, x-rays, and reactions. InterSpec ships with a file data/add_ref_line.xml that defines additional named sources, including useful entries such as "Pu low burnup", "Pu high burnup", "Pu heat source", "U depleted", "U natural", "U 3% enriched", "U-iso", and "Pu-iso". Type any of these names into the "Nuclide" field to use them.

You can also define your own. To do so, copy the shipped file into the apps user data directory (see Help→About InterSpec...→Data for this location), edit it, and restart InterSpec. The user-data copy is loaded after the built-in one, so your custom sources are added to the existing list (rather than replacing it); using the same name as a built-in source will override that built-in entry.

Two source types are supported:
SourceLines: a named list of explicit energy and branching-ratio pairs. Individual lines may optionally be associated with a nuclide (so a peak fit near the line is assigned that nuclide), given a per-line color, or marked to ignore detector efficiency / shielding for the purposes of line display.
NucMixture: a named mixture of nuclides at specified relative activities, with per-nuclide colors and optional age offsets. When entered into this tab the mixture's nuclide lines are displayed, and peaks fit near them are associated with the underlying nuclide (not the mixture name).
For the full list of attributes (default-age, reference-age, age-offset, color, atten, info, nuc, etc.), and worked examples, see the comments in the shipped data/add_ref_line.xml file.

In addition to the reference photopeak lines you enter explicitly in this tool, InterSpec can also display Dynamic Reference Lines that intelligently update as you move your mouse on the spectrum. These lines can be a great way to quickly identify peaks, especially when many sources are present.
Toggle them via Menu→View→"Show Dynamic Reference Lines" (and "Hide Dynamic Reference Lines" to turn off).

The set of sources used for the dynamic lines is partially defined by the file data/dynamic_ref_lines.xml that ships with InterSpec. To customize this set - for example, to add nuclides relevant to your work, change default ages, set per-source colors or shielding, or define custom mixtures - copy the shipped file into the apps user data directory (see Help→About InterSpec...→Data for this location), edit it, and restart InterSpec. If a copy is present in the user data directory it is loaded in place of the built-in one, so your customizations completely replace the default list.

For additional information on creating IndividualSource, SourceLines, and NucMixture entries, and on per-source attributes such as default-age, color, shield-material, shield-thickness, weight, and is-background, please see the data/add_ref_line.xml file.
